name: Literature
description: Explore classic novels, famous authors, poetry, and literary history!

questions.text: Who wrote 'Pride and Prejudice'?
questions.explanation: Jane Austen published 'Pride and Prejudice' in 1813.

questions.text: In which Shakespeare play does the character Hamlet appear?
questions.explanation: Hamlet is the protagonist of Shakespeare's tragedy 'Hamlet', written around 1600.

questions.text: What is the opening line of 'A Tale of Two Cities' by Charles Dickens?
options: It was the best of times, it was the worst of times.
It is a truth universally acknowledged,
questions.explanation: 'It was the best of times, it was the worst of times' is the famous opening of Dickens' novel.

questions.text: Who wrote 'To Kill a Mockingbird'?
questions.explanation: Harper Lee published 'To Kill a Mockingbird' in 1960, winning the Pulitzer Prize.

questions.text: What does the word 'protagonist' mean in literary terms?
options: The main character of the story
questions.explanation: The protagonist is the main character around whom the story centers.

questions.text: Who wrote the epic poem 'The Odyssey'?
questions.explanation: Homer is the ancient Greek poet credited with composing 'The Odyssey'.

questions.text: What is the name of the school Harry Potter attends?
questions.explanation: Harry Potter attends Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry in J.K. Rowling's series.

questions.text: Who wrote '1984'?
questions.explanation: George Orwell published the dystopian novel '1984' in 1949.

questions.text: What is a haiku?
options: A 14-line poem with a rhyme scheme
A poem with no fixed form
A poem that tells a story
questions.explanation: A haiku is a Japanese poetic form consisting of three lines with 5, 7, and 5 syllables.

questions.text: Which Shakespeare play features the characters Romeo and Juliet?
questions.explanation: 'Romeo and Juliet' is one of Shakespeare's most famous tragedies, written around 1595.

questions.text: Who wrote 'The Great Gatsby'?
questions.explanation: F. Scott Fitzgerald published 'The Great Gatsby' in 1925.

questions.text: What literary device involves giving human qualities to non-human things?
questions.explanation: Personification attributes human characteristics to animals, objects, or abstract ideas.

questions.text: Who wrote 'Don Quixote'?
questions.explanation: Miguel de Cervantes wrote 'Don Quixote', published in two parts in 1605 and 1615.

questions.text: In 'Moby-Dick', what is the name of the ship's captain obsessed with the white whale?
questions.explanation: Captain Ahab is the obsessed captain in Herman Melville's 'Moby-Dick' (1851).

questions.text: What is the term for a word that imitates the sound it describes, like 'buzz' or 'crash'?
questions.explanation: Onomatopoeia refers to words that phonetically imitate or resemble the sound they describe.

questions.text: Who wrote 'The Canterbury Tales'?
questions.explanation: Geoffrey Chaucer wrote 'The Canterbury Tales' in the late 14th century.

questions.text: What is the name of the narrator in Herman Melville's 'Moby-Dick'?
questions.explanation: The novel begins with 'Call me Ishmael,' establishing the narrator's name.

questions.text: Which author won the first Nobel Prize in Literature in 1901?
questions.explanation: Sully Prudhomme, a French poet, was awarded the first Nobel Prize in Literature in 1901.

questions.text: What is the term for a narrator who tells a story about themselves?
questions.explanation: A first-person narrator tells the story from their own perspective using 'I'.

questions.text: In which novel does the character Atticus Finch appear?
questions.explanation: Atticus Finch is the lawyer and father in Harper Lee's 'To Kill a Mockingbird'.

questions.text: What is the setting of Shakespeare's play 'Othello'?
questions.explanation: Othello is set primarily in Venice and then Cyprus, reflecting Shakespeare's Venetian settings.

questions.text: Who wrote 'Crime and Punishment'?
questions.explanation: Fyodor Dostoevsky published 'Crime and Punishment' in 1866.

questions.text: What literary term describes the use of clues to hint at future events in a story?
questions.explanation: Foreshadowing is a literary device that provides hints or clues about events that will happen later.

questions.text: Which poet wrote 'The Raven'?
questions.explanation: Edgar Allan Poe published 'The Raven' in 1845, making it one of his most famous works.

questions.text: What is the name of the farm in George Orwell's 'Animal Farm'?
questions.explanation: The story takes place on Manor Farm, which the animals rename Animal Farm after the rebellion.

questions.text: Which Shakespeare play contains the line 'To be, or not to be: that is the question'?
questions.explanation: This famous soliloquy is spoken by Hamlet in Act 3, Scene 1 of Shakespeare's 'Hamlet'.

questions.text: What is a sonnet?
options: A poem with varying line counts
questions.explanation: A sonnet is a 14-line poem, traditionally in iambic pentameter.

questions.text: Who wrote 'Wuthering Heights'?
questions.explanation: Emily Brontë wrote 'Wuthering Heights', published in 1847 under the pseudonym Ellis Bell.

questions.text: In which country was the author Gabriel García Márquez born?
questions.explanation: Gabriel García Márquez was born in Aracataca, Colombia in 1927.

questions.text: What is the term for a story within a story?
questions.explanation: A frame narrative is a story that contains another story within it.

questions.text: Who wrote 'The Catcher in the Rye'?
questions.explanation: J.D. Salinger published 'The Catcher in the Rye' in 1951.

questions.text: What is the literary term for extreme exaggeration used for effect?
questions.explanation: Hyperbole is deliberate exaggeration used for emphasis or comic effect.

questions.text: Which of Shakespeare's plays is the longest?
questions.explanation: Hamlet is Shakespeare's longest play, with approximately 4,000 lines.

questions.text: Who wrote the novel 'Beloved'?
questions.explanation: Toni Morrison wrote 'Beloved' (1987), which won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction.

questions.text: What is a 'bildungsroman'?
questions.explanation: A bildungsroman is a novel that follows the moral and psychological growth of the protagonist.

questions.text: Which poet is famous for the line 'I think that I shall never see / A poem lovely as a tree'?
questions.explanation: Joyce Kilmer wrote the poem 'Trees' (1913), which opens with this famous couplet.

questions.text: In which year was James Joyce's 'Ulysses' first published?
questions.explanation: James Joyce's 'Ulysses' was first published in book form in Paris in 1922.

questions.text: What literary movement did T.S. Eliot and Ezra Pound help define?
questions.explanation: Eliot and Pound were central figures of literary Modernism in the early 20th century.

questions.text: Who wrote 'One Hundred Years of Solitude'?
questions.explanation: Gabriel García Márquez published 'One Hundred Years of Solitude' in 1967.

questions.text: What is the term for a literary work that imitates the style of another work for comic effect?
questions.explanation: A parody imitates the style of another work or author for comic or critical effect.

questions.text: Which Russian author wrote 'Anna Karenina' and 'War and Peace'?
questions.explanation: Leo Tolstoy wrote both 'War and Peace' (1869) and 'Anna Karenina' (1877).

questions.text: What is the term for the repetition of consonant sounds at the beginning of words?
questions.explanation: Alliteration is the repetition of the same consonant sound at the beginning of nearby words.

questions.text: Which author coined the phrase 'the road not taken' in a famous poem?
questions.explanation: Robert Frost wrote 'The Road Not Taken' (1916), which concludes with a reflection on choosing a path.

questions.text: What is the name of the technique that presents a character's thoughts as a continuous flow without logical structure?
questions.explanation: Stream of consciousness attempts to capture the natural flow of a character's thoughts and feelings.

questions.text: Which author won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2016, controversially for song lyrics?
questions.explanation: Bob Dylan won the 2016 Nobel Prize in Literature 'for having created new poetic expressions within the great American song tradition.'

questions.text: In Dante's 'Divine Comedy', who serves as Dante's guide through Hell and Purgatory?
questions.explanation: The Roman poet Virgil serves as Dante's guide through Hell (Inferno) and Purgatory.

questions.text: What is 'magical realism' as a literary genre?
options: Fantasy stories set in alternate worlds
Stories where magical elements coexist naturally with the realistic setting
Science fiction set in ancient times
questions.explanation: Magical realism blends realistic narrative with surreal or magical elements treated as normal within the story.

questions.text: Which Nobel laureate wrote 'The Tin Drum'?
questions.explanation: Günter Grass wrote 'The Tin Drum' (1959) and won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1999.

questions.text: What is an 'unreliable narrator'?
options: A narrator who breaks the fourth wall
A narrator whose credibility is compromised
A narrator who uses stream of consciousness
A narrator who speaks in second person
questions.explanation: An unreliable narrator is one whose account of events is compromised by bias, limited knowledge, or dishonesty.

questions.text: In which Shakespeare play does the witches' prophecy 'All hail, Macbeth! that shalt be king hereafter' appear?
questions.explanation: The three witches deliver this prophecy to Macbeth in Act 1, Scene 3 of 'Macbeth'.
